title: Forget Heart Rate: Scientists Are Now Tracking Your Farts with Smart Underwear
summary: How many times do you fart in a day? Until now, even doctors couldn't tell you. But a team at the University of Maryland has invented "smart underwear" — a quarter-sized sensor that clips onto regular briefs to track flatulence, specifically the hydrogen gas produced by gut microbes. The project, described in Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X, began with a moment of frustration. Microbiologist Brantley Hall and his team were trying to measure gut hydrogen in an oxygen-free chamber, but it wasn't working. "We took the sensor out of the chamber, and we were like, 'Screw it. We're going to try to measure a fart,'" Hall recalls. He stuck it down his pants and let rip. "And the signal was enormous!" That serendipitous toot led to a pilot study with healthy college-age volunteers. The sensors revealed that people average 32 farts a day, but with a striking range: from as few as 4 to as many as 59. When 38 participants ate high-fiber gumdrops, all but two broke wind more frequently, confirming the old playground rhyme. Hall was "shocked by the lack of measurements of intestinal gas." Unlike heart rate or blood pressure, there's no clinical baseline for flatulence. "If you tell [a doctor], 'I'm farting 50 times a day,' they don't have really a baseline to compare that to," he says. To fill this gap, his team launched the Human Flatus Atlas. Volunteers wear the sensor around the clock (except for a 15-minute charge during showers) for 3 to 30 days, photographing all meals via an app. Most forget it's there — participants have played rugby, run 5Ks, and done hours of volleyball. The one forbidden activity? Biking, because the seat presses on the sensor. Early data suggests three farting phenotypes: "zen digesters" who rarely toot even on high-fiber diets, "hydrogen hyperproducers" who toot copiously, and "normal people" in between. The project's popularity exploded: 800 sensors were claimed in days, with over 3,500 on the waiting list. The most and least prolific tooters will even receive 3D-printed plaques. As Hall notes with amusement, "In 2026, we don't know if people are farting at night or not." The Atlas aims to change that, one toot at a time.
why_it_matters: Establishing a scientific baseline for flatulence could help doctors diagnose digestive disorders earlier and reduce stigma around gut health. For young people, understanding that variations are normal can make them more confident discussing their own health and less embarrassed about a universal bodily function.

keywords.explanation: The medical term for passing intestinal gas from the rectum, commonly called farting.
A colorless, odorless gas produced by gut microbes when they break down food, measured by the smart underwear.
The trillions of bacteria and other microorganisms living in the digestive tract that help process food.
The name of the University of Maryland project that maps human farting patterns across a large group of volunteers.
Something good that happens by accident – like the first successful fart measurement that launched this research.
Observable characteristics, such as 'zen digester' or 'hydrogen hyperproducer,' used to classify people by farting frequency.

questions.question: What gas does the smart underwear sensor specifically measure?
What was the average number of farts per day found in the pilot study?
Which activity is forbidden for volunteers wearing the sensor?
What is the official name of the fart‑mapping project?
What food was used to test if fiber increased flatulence?
How many farting 'phenotypes' did the early data suggest?

background_read: While doctors routinely measure heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature, bowel‑gas patterns have been a mystery. The human gut contains trillions of microbes that break down undigested food, releasing hydrogen and other gases in the process.

The University of Maryland, a major public research university in the United States, hosts the lab of microbiologist Brantley Hall. His team published the smart‑underwear study in Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X, a journal focused on new sensing technologies.

Before this project, there was no scientific baseline for flatulence: a patient could tell a doctor they farted 50 times a day and the doctor would have no reference range. The Human Flatus Atlas aims to create that reference, similar to how the body‑mass index chart helps evaluate weight.

The project’s popularity – 800 sensors claimed in days and a waitlist of over 3,500 people – shows how willing people are to share normally private data when they are curious about their own biology. Historically, flatulence has been a taboo topic, but gut health is increasingly recognized as central to overall wellness.
Article_Structure: HOOK: A question nobody could answer – how many times do you fart in a day? – is now addressed by smart underwear.
INVENTION: A quarter‑sized sensor clips onto regular briefs and measures hydrogen gas produced by gut microbes.
└─ ORIGIN STORY: A failed experiment in an oxygen‑free box led a researcher to try the sensor on himself; the fart signal was 'enormous.'
PILOT STUDY: Healthy college students wore the sensor; average 32 farts per day, with a startling range from 4 to 59.
└─ EXPERIMENT: High‑fiber gumdrops caused all but two participants to break wind more often.
THE PROBLEM: Unlike heart rate, flatulence has no clinical baseline, so doctors cannot judge if a patient's farting is normal.
├─ METHOD: Volunteers wear the sensor 24/7 (except a 15‑minute charge during showers) and photograph every meal.
├─ RULES: Biking is forbidden because the seat presses on the sensor; all other activities are allowed.
└─ PHENOTYPES: Early data suggests three types – 'zen digesters' who rarely toot, 'hydrogen hyperproducers' who toot a lot, and normal people.
ENGAGEMENT: 800 sensors claimed in days, over 3,500 on the waiting list; the most and least prolific tooters will get 3D‑printed plaques.
CLOSING QUOTE: 'In 2026, we don't know if people are farting at night or not.' The Atlas hopes to answer that.

perspectives.description: For individuals with irritable bowel syndrome or other digestive issues, hard data on gas production could validate symptoms and guide dietary changes. The sensor is unobtrusive and may normalize a natural process, reducing embarrassment.

Privacy experts worry that continuous recording of intimate bodily functions could be misused by insurance companies or employers. Additionally, some people may find the very idea of a fart sensor humiliating, which could limit adoption.

Fart frequency likely depends on diet, microbiome composition, and metabolism, but correlation does not mean causation. The Atlas will produce big data, but scientists still need to sort out whether a given pattern signals disease or just a love of beans.

Future versions of the sensor might be built into smart clothing and paired with phone apps that provide real‑time dietary feedback. This could shift the focus of gut health from treating symptoms to personalized, prevention‑oriented daily management.
